coredata.dc:title: Dual interfacial modification to improve the performance of CsPbBr3 perovskite solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xiaoyan
Yang, Meili
Hou, Shaochuan
Yan, Jiahao
Dong, Jingjing
Zou, Li
Xing, Jie
Liu, Hao
Hao, Huiying
coredata.dc:description: Although CsPbBr3 perovskite solar cells (PSCs) have excellent thermal and humidity stability, the narrow spectral absorption range and severe charge recombination within the CsPbBr3 film and device interfaces result in a low power conversion efficiency (PCE). In this work, self-synthesized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and poly(3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT) are introduced to modify the m-TiO2/CsPbBr3 and CsPbBr3/carbon interfaces of CsPbBr3 PSCs, respectively. It is found that the carrier extraction and transfer are improved enormously after GQDs and P3HT dual modifications, which can be attributed to the passivated trap states, suppressed charge recombination, and reduced the interfacial energy barrier. Ultimately, for the carbon-based CsPbBr3 PSCs, a champion PCE as high as 9.74% with outstanding short-circuit current density (JSC) of 9.36 mA cm-2 and open-circuit voltage (VOC) of 1.42 V has been achieved, and the device stability has also been improved greatly. Such a strategy contributes to improving the performance of CsPbBr3 PSCs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Perovskite solar cells
CsPbBr3
Interface modification
GQDs
P3HT

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the perovskite solar cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM image of GQDs. The upper and lower insets represent the Raman curves of GQDs and the photograph of the GQDs solution.
(a) J-V curves under dark conditions for the electron-only devices. (b) The dark J-V curves of PSCs with and without GQDs.
Schematic of the (a) cross-sectional structure and (b) energy level alignment of CsPbBr3 PSCs.
XPS survey spectra of (a) O 1s, (b) Ti 2p for m-TiO2 film and m-TiO2/GQDs film, (c) S 2P, (d) Pb 4f for CsPbBr3 film and CsPbBr3/P3HT film.

The SEM surface images of the (a) pristine CsPbBr3 film, (c) CsPbBr3 film on the GQDs modification layer, (d) CsPbBr3 film covered by P3HT modification layer. (b) The cross-sectional SEM image of the pristine CsPbBr3 film. AFM images of CsPbBr3 film (e) without and (f) with P3HT modification layer.
(a) Steady-state PL and (b) TRPL spectra of FTO/c-TiO2/m-TiO2/CsPbBr3/carbon, FTO/c-TiO2/m-TiO2/GQDs/CsPbBr3/carbon and FTO/c-TiO2/m-TiO2/GQDs/CsPbBr3/P3HT/carbon.

The (a) J-V curves, (b) transient VOC measurement spectra, (c) histograms of the conversion efficiencies, (d) stability of PSCs without modification, with GQDs modification, and with dual modifications.

head.ce:title: Dual interfacial modification to improve the performance of CsPbBr3 perovskite solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, China University of Geosciences, Beijing, 100083, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Science, China University of Geosciences, Beijing, 100083, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Although CsPbBr3 perovskite solar cells (PSCs) have excellent thermal and humidity stability, the narrow spectral absorption range and severe charge recombination within the CsPbBr3 film and device interfaces result in a low power conversion efficiency (PCE). In this work, self-synthesized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and poly(3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT) are introduced to modify the m-TiO2/CsPbBr3 and CsPbBr3/carbon interfaces of CsPbBr3 PSCs, respectively. It is found that the carrier extraction and transfer are improved enormously after GQDs and P3HT dual modifications, which can be attributed to the passivated trap states, suppressed charge recombination, and reduced the interfacial energy barrier. Ultimately, for the carbon-based CsPbBr3 PSCs, a champion PCE as high as 9.74% with outstanding short-circuit current density (JSC) of 9.36 mA cm-2 and open-circuit voltage (VOC) of 1.42 V has been achieved, and the device stability has also been improved greatly. Such a strategy contributes to improving the performance of CsPbBr3 PSCs.

ce:section.ce:para: Perovskite materials have become a research hotspot and are widely used in solar cells in recent years, due to their strong absorption coefficient, wide wavelength range, long carrier life and diffusion length [1]. The power conversion efficiency (PCE) of perovskite solar cells (PSCs) is developing rapidly, from 3.8% reported in 2009 to 25.5% certified recently [2–4]. However, organic and inorganic hybrid PSCs are particularly sensitive to light, heat, and water. High temperature and high humidity can cause the decomposition of perovskite materials and affect the performance of devices [5]. So, organic and inorganic hybrid PSCs usually need to be prepared in a nitrogen-filled glove box. All-inorganic PSCs, in which Cs+ ions take the place of organic cations such as methylammonium (MA+) and formazan (FA+), have attracted wide attention in recent years [6–8]. Among the all-inorganic PSCs, fully brominated CsPbBr3 PSCs present the best stability and can be prepared in the air without humidity and temperature control.

Although CsPbBr3 PSCs have excellent thermal and humidity stability, the narrow spectral absorption range and severe charge recombination within the CsPbBr3 film and device interface result in a far lower power conversion efficiency (PCE) than organic and inorganic hybrid PSCs [9]. Interface engineering is a highly feasible method to improve the performance of PSCs. It can make better contact between the perovskite active layer and carrier transport layer, and then enhance the carrier extraction, reduce the charge recombination, regulate the band offset, decrease the interface resistance, finally improve the performance of devices. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are excellent materials to modify the electron transport layer (ETL)/perovskite interface due to their superior properties, for example, high conductivity and tunable band gap [10]. Duan et al. have incorporated the GQDs on the surface of TiO2 ETL in inorganic CsPbBr3 PSCs, and the PCE is increased from 7.54% to 9.72%. This efficiency enhancement arises from a reduced interfacial potential barrier and increased electron extraction [11]. Poly(3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT) is widely used as hole transport layer (HTL) and modified layer in perovskite/carbon interface due to its outstanding properties, low cost and ease of fabrication [12–15]. Wang et al. have utilized P3HT as CsPbBr3/carbon interface modifying materials, and the PCE of devices is increased by 27% [16], however, the PCE of the device remains low, only 6.49%.

In this paper, GQDs were successfully synthesized by a solvothermal method, and spin-coated on the surface of mesoscopic TiO2 (m-TiO2) to modify the m-TiO2/CsPbBr3 interface. At the same time, P3HT was introduced to modify the CsPbBr3/carbon interface. After the dual interfacial modification, we found that the carrier recombination was suppressed, while the carrier extraction and transportation were promoted, due to the reduced trap state density and interfacial energy barrier. Ultimately, a champion PCE as high as 9.74% with an outstanding short-circuit current density (JSC) of 9.36 mA cm-2 and open-circuit voltage (VOC) of 1.42 V was achieved after GQDs and P3HT modification, and the device stability was also improved greatly.

All reagents were purchased from Aladdin and used without further purification, including N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.8%), Ethanol absolute (99.5%), Chlorobenzene (99%), Methanol (99.8%), Isopropyl titanate. Graphite Oxide Powder was purchased from Aladdin, Lead bromide (PbBr2), Cesium bromide (CsBr), and P3HT were obtained in the Polymer Light Technology Corp (Xi'an, China).

GQDs were prepared by dispersing 135 mg of Graphite Oxide powders into 15 mL of DMF, and then ultrasonication for 30 min. After that, the dispersed solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 8 h [17]. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was transferred to the centrifuge tube, centrifuged at 12000 r/min for 5 min. Finally, the supernatant was removed.

The etched fluorine-incorporated tin oxide (FTO) glass substrate was cleaned with detergent, isopropanol, ethanol, deionized water, and isopropanol for 15 min respectively. Then, the substrate was treated by plasma cleaner at 40 W for 30 s. Next, a solution of isopropyl titanate and hydrochloric acid in anhydrous ethanol was spin-coated at 3000 rpm for the 30 s and annealed at 150 °C for 15 min, and then sintered at 500 °C for 30 min to obtain the compact TiO2 (c-TiO2). After that, the m-TiO2 was deposited from a mixed solution of TiO2 paste and anhydrous ethanol (with the weight ratio of 1:4) by spin-coating at 5000 rpm for 30 s, and annealed in a muffle furnace at 500 °C for 30 min. The GQDs were spin-coated on m-TiO2 film at 2000 rpm for 30 s as interface modification layer, annealed at 80 °C for 15 min, and then treated by plasma cleaner at 40 W for 30 s.

The CsPbBr3 perovskite layer was prepared by a multi-step spin-coating method. Firstly, the FTO/c-TiO2/m-TiO2/GQDs substrate was preheated on the hot plate at 80 °C, and then 1 M PbBr2/DMF solution was deposited onto the preheated substrate by spin-coating at 2000 rpm for 30 s, followed by annealing at 90 °C for 30 min. Secondly, 0.07 M CsBr/methanol solution was spin-coated on above PbBr2 film at 2000 rpm for 30 s and annealed at 250 °C for 5 min to remove the residual solvent. The spin-coating and annealing of CsBr were repeated for several times until a bright yellow CsPbBr3 film was obtained. The P3HT as an interface modification layer was deposited on CsPbBr3 film by spin-coating the P3HT/chlorobenzene solution with a concentration of 10 mg/mL at 3000 rpm for 30 s and followed by annealing at 180 °C for 5 min. Finally, 0.1 cm-2 carbon electrodes are deposited on top of the P3HT layer by using screen printing, and dried at 120 °C for 15 min. The active area of the solar cells is 0.1 cm-2. Schematic diagram of the preparation process is shown in Fig. S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the perovskite solar cells
ce:section.ce:para: Transmission electron microscope (TEM) was used to characterize the morphology and distribution of GQDs. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) was used to characterize the morphology of the CsPbBr3 films. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to study the passivation mechanism. The structure and crystal quality of CsPbBr3 films were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) on D8 focus. Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra and Time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) were used to study the effect of GQDs and P3HT modification layers on charge transfer and extraction. Current density-voltage (J-V), dark J-V, and space-charge limited-current (SCLC) curves were measured by a Keithley 2400 source meter with a solar simulator (Zolixss150) under AM 1.5G, 100 mW cm−2 illumination. The transient VOC measurement was measured by an oscilloscope (Tektronix, MDO3052).

The GQDs have been prepared by the one-step solvothermal method [17]. The TEM image was utilized to present the morphology and distribution of GQDs, as shown in Fig. 1 , from which we can see that the spots disperse evently and exhibit an average diameter of 5 nm. The Raman spectrum was then used to prove that the spots in the TEM image are indeed GQDs. As shown in the upper inset of Fig. 1, there are the typical carbon-related frequency bands at 1346 cm-1 (D band) and 1579 cm-1 (G band), consistent with previous reports [18,19]. Combined TEM and Raman results above, we confirm that we have successfully synthesized GQDs, and the GQDs solution is shown in the lower inset of Fig. 1.

To authenticate the improvement of charge transport behaviors and the reduction of defect states, the trap-state density was estimated according to the SCLC curves. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the voltage at the kink point is the trap-filled limit voltage (VTFL), the linear current-voltage relationship is presented before the voltage at the kink point, representing an Ohmic response, while the quadratic relationship is shown when the bias voltage exceeds the kink point, indicating a filled trap state [20]. The defect state density (Ntrap) can be calculated according to the following formula using the VTFL [8,21,22]:
(1)
N
t r a p
= 2
ε r
ε 0
V
T F L
/ q
L 2
where L is the thickness of the perovskite film (430 nm), εr represents the dielectric constant of CsPbBr3, which is approximately equal to 8.6 [23,24], ε0 refers to the vacuum permittivity, and q represents the elementary charge. After calculation, the results display that the Ntrap remarkably decreases from 4.89 × 1015 cm-3 for the pristine device to 3.70 × 1015 cm-3 for the device modified with GQDs. The reduction of defect state density indicates that the traps and defects on the m-TiO2/CsPbBr3 interface were passivated effectively by the GQDs modification layer [25], which will promote charge extraction, accelerate carrier transfer, and improve the JSC of PSCs. To deeply understand the influence of GQDs-modification on charge recombination, the J-V curves of devices have been measured under dark conditions, and the results are shown in Fig. 2b. We can see that the dark current density of GQDs modified devices is much lower than that of the pristine devices, which means that GQDs play an important role in decreasing the leakage path of the current, and promote photo-generated currents to pass through the devices directly, rather than be shunted [5]. The much more superior contact between the m-TiO2 and CsPbBr3 film can be attributed to the reduced trap states and suppressed charge recombination after GQDs modification, which is consistent with previous SCLC results and will improve the JSC of PSCs.

Although the GQDs modification can passivate the traps and defects, reduce the interfacial energy barrier, and promote the extraction of photo-generated electrons in the m-TiO2/CsPbBr3 interface, the interface issues still exist in the CsPbBr3/carbon interface. To solve this problem, we try to modify the CsPbBr3/carbon interface with P3HT by passivating the interfacial defects and regulating the energy barrier, reducing the hole loss, and further improving the performance of the devices.

The cross-sectional structure and energy level alignment of CsPbBr3 PSCs are schematically shown in Fig. 3 , where the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) values were -3.8 eV for GQDs [11], between LUMO of TiO2 (-4.2 eV) and CsPbBr3 (-3.3 eV), which can reduce the interfacial energy barrier and promote the extraction of photo-generated electrons, and this is another reason why the JSC can be increased after GQDs modification. From the cross-sectional structure in Fig. 3b, we can see that the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of P3HT is -5.1 eV [16], and the valence band maximum (VBM) of CsPbBr3 and the work function of carbon are -5.6 eV and -5.0 eV [11], respectively. Therefore, a more appropriate energy level alignment would appear at the CsPbBr3/carbon interface if P3HT was inserted. Furthermore, after introducing the P3HT modification layer, we can acquire a smooth and flat CsPbBr3 surface, which is beneficial to the contact between CsPbBr3 films and carbon electrodes, and will improve the collection efficiency of the holes.

XPS was employed to study the passivation mechanism of modification layers, and the corresponding results were shown in Fig. 4 . As shown in Fig. 4a, the peak position of O 1s shifted to the lower binding energy direction, which indicates that the modification of GQDs could passivate the oxygen vacancies in the m-TiO2 layer effectively. The same phenomenon can be observed in the spectra of Ti 2p for the m-TiO2 films with and without GQDs modification, as shown in Fig. 4b. In Fig. 4c, we can clearly see the peaks of S 2p for the CsPbBr3 film with P3HT modification, implying that P3HT was successfully deposited on the CsPbBr3 film. Furthermore, the Pb 4f peaks shift towards lower binding energy after P3HT modification as shown in Fig. 4d, indicating that the S atoms in P3HT could passivate the interfacial defects by bonding with Pb atoms at the CsPbBr3/P3HT interface.

Fig. 5 a and b presents the top-view and cross-sectional SEM images of the pristine CsPbBr3 film without interface modification. We can see that the pristine CsPbBr3 film presents large grain size, small grain boundary, uniform and full coverage. Furthermore, no pinhole or void was observed. After GQDs modification, as shown in Fig. 5c, the CsPbBr3 film on the GQDs modification layer is no different from the pristine CsPbBr3 film in Fig. 5a, which implies that the introduction of the GQDs layer will not affect the quality of the CsPbBr3 film. Covered by P3HT modification layer, as shown in Fig. 5d, we can see that the surface of CsPbBr3 film is blurred compared with the SEM images shown in Fig. 5a and c, while the perovskite grains and grain boundaries still can be seen clearly through the P3HT layer, indicating that the P3HT layer is very thin, which can be further confirmed by the AFM results in Fig. 5f. Therefore, the thin layer of P3HT acts as the modified layer rather than the HTL [16,26].

XRD patterns of the pristine CsPbBr3 film, CsPbBr3 film on the GQDs modification layer, and CsPbBr3 film covered by the P3HT modification layer are shown in Fig. S2 to clarify the crystal quality. The characteristic diffraction peaks at around 15.32°, 21.80°, 26.54°, 30.83° and 34.69° correspond to the diffraction planes (100), (110), (111), (200), (210) and (211) of the perovskite films, respectively, suggesting that an impurity-free crystal structure of CsPbBr3 perovskite has been obtained [8,27,28]. Meanwhile, several peaks centered at 11.73°, 24.27°, 29.54° and 33.73° are also detected, which corresponds to the PbBr2-riched phase of CsPb2Br5 [11], implying there is a little bit of PbBr2 residual for all of the three perovskite films. In addition, it was found that all of the three perovskite films exhibited the same crystalline phase, and the characteristic diffraction peaks of CsPbBr3 films on the GQDs modification layer and covered by the P3HT modification layer did not shift compared with the pristine CsPbBr3 film, suggesting that GQDs or P3HT modification layer did not affect the crystal structure of CsPbBr3 films.

To get insight into the effect of GQDs and P3HT modification layers on charge transfer and extraction at m-TiO2/CsPbBr3 and CsPbBr3/carbon interfaces, steady PL and TRPL measurements are measured, and the corresponding results are presented in Fig. 6 a and b, respectively. Fig. 6a portrays the PL quenching on CsPbBr3 films deposited on m-TiO2 without modification, with GQDs modification, and with dual modifications. The PL quenching is evidence of carrier charge separation at the interface. The more efficient PL quenching after interface modification suggests an enhanced electron/hole extraction ability. We can see from Fig. 6a that the PL peak intensity for the sample with GQDs modification is much lower than the sample without modification, indicating that the electron extraction capability is improved greatly after GQDs modification, which can be attributed to the passivated trap states, suppressed charge recombination, and reduced the interfacial energy barrier after GQDs modification [29,30], as proved by our previous results. The PL peak intensity continues to decline after dual modifications are introduced, implying that both electron and hole extraction abilities are enhanced significantly after dual modifications. Moreover, the PL peaks show a slight blue shift from 527 nm to 524 nm after GQDs modification, and further to 520 nm after dual modifications, indicating the passivation of the trap states in the CsPbBr3 films after modifications. The passivation effect of GQDs modification has been proved by previous SCLC and dark J-V results. For the passivation effect of the P3HT layer, it was reported that the S atoms in P3HT could bond with Pb atoms at the CsPbBr3/P3HT interface, and then passivated the interfacial defects, which is consistent with the XPS results above [16,31]. To study the charge extraction dynamics and carrier lifetimes, the TRPL results are presented in Fig. 6b, which can be well fitted by a biexponential function, and the corresponding lifetimes τ1 and τ2 are summarized in the inset of Fig. 6b. The faster decay component τ1 indicates trap-related nonradiative recombination, while the slower decay component τ2 indicates radiative recombination [21,32]. The average carrier lifetime τave of 109.8, 97.6, and 92.8 ns are obtained for the sample without modification, with GQDs modification, and with dual modification, respectively. The carrier lifetimes are sharply decreased after the introduction of GQDs and P3HT, illustrating the electron and hole injection from the perovskite layer to the transport layer is faster, which is mainly attributed to the high conductivity and well-matched energy levels of GQDs and P3HT modification layers.

We prepared devices with a structure of FTO/c-TiO2/m-TiO2/CsPbBr3/carbon in the air to explore the effects of GQDs and P3HT modifications on device performance. Fig. 7 a shows the J-V curves of PSCs without modification, with GQDs modification, and with dual modifications under the condition of AM 1.5G (100 mW cm-2) solar illumination, the inset of which is the corresponding photovoltaic parameters. We can see that the PCE of the PSCs has been improved remarkably from 6.25% to 8.24% after GQDs modification, and further improved to 9.74% after GQDs and P3HT dual modifications. The enhancement of the PCE after GQDs modification is mainly caused by the increase of JSC, as shown in the inset of Fig. 7a, which can be attributed to the boosted electron extraction capability caused by the passivated trap states, suppressed charge recombination, and reduced the interfacial energy barrier after GQDs modification, as stated earlier. While the improvement of the PCE after dual modifications, compared with the device with GQDs modification, is mainly caused by the increase of VOC, as shown in the inset of Fig. 7a because a more appropriate energy level alignment is obtained at the CsPbBr3/carbon interface by introducing P3HT, which can reduce the loss of VOC. The dual-modified device presents the optimal performance with a champion PCE of 9.74%, JSC of 9.36 mA cm-2, VOC of 1.42 V, and fill factor (FF) of 73%.

The low hysteresis in the PSCs with interface modifications is a direct consequence of the rapid response times of these devices [33,34], as seen in the transient VOC measurement under light/dark cycling in Fig. 7b. There is a much faster response for the champion dual-modified device in comparison with the device without modification or with GQDs modification. The first dark/light transition exhibits that the VOC of the device with dual modifications reaches a steady-state maximum in less than 2s, while the device without modification takes more than 5s for the VOC to stabilize. As reported, trap and defect states at the interfaces of PSCs may lead to the slow response [34], and they can be greatly passivated by GQDs and P3HT modifications so that fewer carriers are trapped and released, resulting in a much faster response, which is in consistent with previous steady PL and TRPL results. Fig. 7c displays the statistical histogram of PCE for 45 individual devices, illustrating the reproducibility and reliability of our results. The excellent long-term stability of PSCs is an important issue for commercialization. Therefore, the stability of devices is measured over 35 days in the ambient air without encapsulation. As shown in Fig. 7d, the cell with GQDs and P3HT dual modifications exhibit more excellent long-term stability than the devices only with GQDs modification or without modification, which is attributed to the passivated interface trap and defect states and improved interfacial compatibility.

In summary, we have offered an effective approach to improve the performance of CsPbBr3 PSCs via introducing GQDs and P3HT to modify the m-TiO2/CsPbBr3 and CsPbBr3/carbon interfaces, respectively. It was found that the carrier extraction and transfer were improved enormously after interface modification, especially after GQDs and P3HT dual modifications, which could be attributed to the passivated trap states, suppressed charge recombination, and reduced the interfacial energy barrier. By virtue of GQDs and P3HT dual modifications, a champion PCE as high as 9.74% was obtained with the structure of FTO/c-TiO2/m-TiO2/GQDs/CsPbBr3/P3HT/carbon, which was 56% higher than the PCE of the device without interface modification. Moreover, the PSCs with dual modifications displayed remarkable long-term stability in the ambient air for 840 h. Such a strategy contributes to improving the performance of CsPbBr3 PSCs.

Xiaoyan Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - Original Draft. Shaochuan Hou: Methodology, Writing - Original Draft. Jiahao Yan: Methodology, Writing - Original Draft. Jingjing Dong: Validation, Writing - Review & Editing. Li Zou: Investigation. Meili Yang: Investigation. Jie Xing: Project administration. Hao Liu: Project administration. Huiying Hao: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the “Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities” (Grand No. 2652019121) and “National Natural Science Foundation of China” (Grand No. 11404293).
